comments.text: Seen a few folks astonished by my lack of support for ChatGPT and similar. Because AI, right? Yeah, no. What we have right now is glorified autocorrect. Glorified autocorrect that takes the energy of an entire city to do what it does, plus half of Lake Erie to cool down while it's doing it.
These are my favorite kinds of AI search results. I'd like to think that one percent of Lake Erie vanishes with each return like this.

Here in the Midwest, data centers threaten to suck up fresh water from our Great Lakes. Just one of may examples includes a Microsoft plant now under construction near Racine, WI that is expected to use upwards of 8 MILLION gallons a day. A day! That is not okay.
Data centre proponents say facility will run on solar power and all of Lake Superior

look, laugh now. but i firmly believe that by investing a mere $3 trillion into AI and draining 2 of the 5 Great Lakes, we may one day create a machine that successfully dispenses snacks and drinks in exchange for cash

Look it might be using more power than Indonesia and sucking Lake Michigan dry and failing at almost every task companies put it to but at least it makes you more susceptible to people trying to take your money

i know i'm beating a dead horse but it doesn't take an advanced degree in hydrogeolgy to understand that water usage for a data center in phoenix might be a slightly different issue in scale and impact than it is for one on the banks of lake michigan so perhaps pooh poohing the issue is dumb af

And it’s killing our fresh water supplies. People don’t realize how much water it takes to cool these machines. They should not be draining our Great Lakes to do this. #AIIsKillingOurPlanet Please avoid using it if you can. www.forbes.com/sites/cindyg....
no Ai. no green screen. just a man and his floatie and Lake Erie.

Saline Township, a small Michigan town with a 3K population, is going up against OpenAI and Oracle's Stargate. The experts warn the data centers' undisclosed water and electricity usage could strain resources, especially near Lake Michigan. #ProudBlue #DataCenters #Michigan

we’re gonna be confronting this issue for the rest of our lives, imo, so no reason to get mega-hot about one example, but: this is about tech trying to get a foot in with the Great Lakes water supply, not much different from FoxConn

These data centers are coming to the Great Lakes basin because of our water. Humans should be concerned about the impact on the Great Lakes and explain the huge difference between gallons/day used and gallons/day discharged back into the lake. How will this affect me? 🌊🌊

You'd think a program built off stealing all the data on the internet would understand this is from a well-known quote from the game, "When you lack a silver spoon you fashion one of steel" but nooope. Enough processing power to drain Lake Superior but it still can't figure this out. lol.

If we drain the Great Lakes to accommodate the data centers' cooling, America AND Canada will experience a fresh water shortage like you've never seen. I hate these data centers more than I've ever hated anything.
The fact that Apple Intelligence is going to happen mostly on-device should give a bit of pause to the thesis that AI means draining the Great Lakes to cool city-sized data centers.

My home state is literally fighting for its life to stop them from building data centers that will straight up drain the great lakes. Flint aint bout to be the only place with fucked water yet "haha make funny thing look" is all ppl care about. Fuck you and fuck ai
A recent report warns the Great Lakes region is not prepared for the unprecedented, growing demand from data centers and other water-heavy users.

Our local Midwest town is currently fighting a data center. Being near the headwaters of the St.Louis, several waters that lead into the Mississippi and Lake Superior itself make the whole water thing super concerning. They’ve assured the public they will be HE-3 instead…something they can’t afford

"Large data centers, many devoted to researching artificial intelligence, are expected to use more than 150 billion gallons of water across the U.S. over the next five years, according to the advocacy organization Alliance for the Great Lakes. That’s enough water to supply 4.6 million homes."

Some of the most important water sources in the USA now have Data mills.
The Great Lakes, Texas Aquifers, and the Umatilla Basin here in Oregon. Amazon recently lost a case but only have to pay a paltry $20.5 million settlement for nitrate runoff destroying groundwater.

so a few minutes of playing with #deepseek it seems like its great innovation might just be that it reasons out the query before answering? like did we drain lake superior and allow bros to grift for billions because they couldn't concieve of internal monologue? ahahahahahahaha
I want data centers to not use all the water in the Great Lakes. I want rent control and no new apartments, but my friends should all be able to move here.
(after draining the entirety of lake superior) i can't wait to generate an ai image of elvis meeting nixon

Chef: “I wish I could cook poached eggs more consistently…”
Tech: “AI can help! See? It just made eggs! They’re also technically food! We’re getting better at that!”
Chef: “But—“
Tech: “Soon we won’t need chefs or even farms. We’ll have AI!”
Voice (distant): “Hey, where the fuck did Lake Erie go??”
The people at companies that post jobs advertising remote librarian work but the job is just a $20 p/h AI data training job should be put into a cannon and fired out over Lake Michigan.

It makes it clear the partisan hackery that’s going on here. It’s not about uncovering fraud, it’s about a racist ice agenda and unseating the guy who called Elon a dipshit. What has Demuth promised the people boosting this? Data centers with Lake Superior water? Tesla battery mines?

Shoving AI and Data Centers down our throats, draining the Great Lakes, destabilizing our electric grids and jacking up rates for electricity so that corporations can save money on advertising. Capitalism is broken.

Amazon's new $11B 1,200-acre data center in Indiana, near Lake Michigan. Operational as of November, it will use 2.2 gigawatts of power and 300 million gallons of water per year to run and train AI models from Anthropic.
Stop being so shortsighted. Technology takes time to develop. I'm sure if we boil off the Great Lakes to cool a server farm the size of Nebraska we can easily get that accuracy up to 70%
We have to keep AI data centers away from the Great Lakes

Different thought about datacenters being built all over: companies from outside the great lakes basin coming in to build these things near the great lakes feels like a violation of the spirit of the great lakes compact, if not the letter of the law. It's not free for the taking.
Capitalism wants to drain the Great Lakes so that they can make more computers that can't actually compute anything

People are still droning on about the ai using all the water. I’m pleased to say the Great Lakes are still great

You should look at a map of the planned data centers! Around all the water sources!! ALL! The Great Lakes, rivers, oceans… all the water!

Behind the sleek promises of AI is a thirst for water. Data centres consume huge amounts, straining supplies and fuelling clashes with farmers and Indigenous communities. In Canada, dozens of facilities are clustered near the Great Lakes, raising questions about how much more the ecosystem can bear.

Honestly ecstatic that residents of one of our smaller localities in Wisconsin pushed back and prevented construction of a data center. These AI Bros are coming for the Great Lakes and I’m happy to see them fail in their ventures.

Anyway. Curiosity killed the cat, or something, and computers are great but also pretty bad for the environment. As a Great Lakes lover (and liver), fuck all that. Every time you use ChatGPT or whatev, please donate to your local environmental org because they will need help as the tech balloons.

We don’t need to build data centers in space (dumb) we need to build them in the Pacific Northwest and Great Lakes region where there is cold water and good infrastructure. It’s not complicated: Just do whatever Norway does.
I remember watching i-Robot and seeing Lake Michigan was all dried up, and I didn't fully get the message. Seeing these awful effects of these data centers, I get that part of the movie now.
